infobox.value: [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")
[Nancy L. Hill](/w/index.php?title=Nancy_L._Hill&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nancy L. Hill (page does not exist)")

[University of Michigan Law School](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School "University of Michigan Law School") ([JD](/wiki/Juris_Doctor "Juris Doctor")) [Dickinson College](/wiki/Dickinson_College "Dickinson College") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts"))

(born September 12, 1957) is a former member of the [Arizona State Senate](/wiki/Arizona_State_Senate "Arizona State Senate"). He served in the Senate from January 1993 through January 2001, representing district 18.[[1]](#cite_note-1): vii[[2]](#cite_note-2): vii[[3]](#cite_note-3): vii[[4]](#cite_note-4): vii The amendment to the [Arizona Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Arizona "Constitution of Arizona") which limited politicians to serving four consecutive terms in either house was passed in 1992, preventing Spitzer from running again in November 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 2000, Spitzer was elected to the [Arizona Corporation Commission](/wiki/Arizona_Corporation_Commission "Arizona Corporation Commission"), where he served from January 2001 to July 2006.

["Session laws, State of Arizona, 1993 Volume 1, Forty-First Legislature, First Regular Session, Chapters 1 to 184"](http://azmemory.azlibrary.gov/digital/collection/azsession/id/69/rec/2). State of Arizona. Retrieved November 19, 2018.

["Session laws, State of Arizona, 1997 Volume 1, Forty-Third Legislature, First Regular Session, Chapters 1 to 146"](http://azmemory.azlibrary.gov/digital/collection/azsession/id/43/rec/1). State of Arizona. Retrieved December 4, 2018.

["Session laws, State of Arizona, 1999 Volume 1, Forty-Fourth Legislature, First Regular Session, Chapters 1 to 223"](http://azmemory.azlibrary.gov/digital/collection/azsession/id/116/rec/1). State of Arizona. Retrieved December 12, 2018.

first_paragraph: **Marc Spitzer** (born September 12, 1957) is a former member of the [Arizona State Senate](/wiki/Arizona_State_Senate "Arizona State Senate"). He served in the Senate from January 1993 through January 2001, representing district 18.[[1]](#cite_note-1): vii[[2]](#cite_note-2): vii[[3]](#cite_note-3): vii[[4]](#cite_note-4): vii The amendment to the [Arizona Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Arizona "Constitution of Arizona") which limited politicians to serving four consecutive terms in either house was passed in 1992, preventing Spitzer from running again in November 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 2000, Spitzer was elected to the [Arizona Corporation Commission](/wiki/Arizona_Corporation_Commission "Arizona Corporation Commission"), where he served from January 2001 to July 2006.

**Marc Spitzer** (born September 12, 1957) is a former member of the [Arizona State Senate](/wiki/Arizona_State_Senate "Arizona State Senate"). He served in the Senate from January 1993 through January 2001, representing district 18.[[1]](#cite_note-1): vii[[2]](#cite_note-2): vii[[3]](#cite_note-3): vii[[4]](#cite_note-4): vii The amendment to the [Arizona Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Arizona "Constitution of Arizona") which limited politicians to serving four consecutive terms in either house was passed in 1992, preventing Spitzer from running again in November 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 2000, Spitzer was elected to the [Arizona Corporation Commission](/wiki/Arizona_Corporation_Commission "Arizona Corporation Commission"), where he served from January 2001 to July 2006.

Nominated by President G.W. Bush in June 2006 and confirmed by unanimous vote of the US Senate on July 14, 2006, Spitzer also served as Commissioner for the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission") from July 21, 2006 – December 14, 2011.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Currently, Spitzer is an attorney at [Steptoe & Johnson LLP](/wiki/Steptoe_%26_Johnson "Steptoe & Johnson") in Washington, DC. He resides in McLean, VA.
